sections.title: New complaints procedures for Information Regulator
sections.summary: This section adds Chapter 1A to Part 4 of the POPIA, detailing the complaints procedures for the Information Regulator. It specifies that requesters and third parties may submit complaints to the Information Regulator after exhausting internal appeal procedures. It outlines the timeframes for complaints (180 days), the types of decisions that can be challenged, and the procedures for handling complaints including investigation, settlement, and enforcement actions. The Information Regulator has powers similar to the High Court for investigations and can issue enforcement notices for non-compliance.

sections.title: Notification of security compromises
sections.summary: Where there are reasonable grounds to believe that personal information has been accessed or acquired by an unauthorized person, the responsible party must notify the Regulator and the data subject (if identity can be established) as soon as reasonably possible. The notification must include details of the compromise, intended corrective actions, and recommendations for the data subject. The responsible party may delay notification to the data subject if a public body or Regulator determines it would impede a criminal investigation.

sections.heading: (1) The powers, duties and functions of the Regulator in terms of this Act are—
sections.title: Regulator's powers and duties
sections.summary: This section details the Information Regulator's powers and duties, including providing education on data protection, monitoring compliance, conducting research, handling complaints, publishing reports, and facilitating cross-border cooperation. It specifies the Regulator's role in ensuring data protection across public and private sectors.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines the regulatory framework for oversight of data transfers, including enforcement mechanisms and cross-border cooperation, critical for assessing compliance with international data flows.

requirements.text: Regulator provides education on lawful processing of personal information
Regulator monitors compliance by public and private bodies with the Act

Regulator assesses bodies for compliance with data protection conditions

Regulator facilitates cross-border cooperation in privacy law enforcement

sections.source_quote: 40. (1) The powers, duties and functions of the Regulator in terms of this Act are— (a) to provide education by— (i) promoting an understanding and acceptance of the conditions for the lawful processing of personal information and of the objects of those conditions; (ii) undertaking educational programmes, for the purpose of promoting the protection of personal information, on the Regulator's own behalf or in co-operation with other persons or authorities acting on behalf of the Regulator; (iii) making public statements in relation to any matter affecting the protection of the personal information of a data subject or of any class of data subjects; (iv) giving advice to data subjects in the exercise of their rights; and (v) providing advice, upon request or on its own initiative, to a Minister or a public or private body on their obligations under the provisions, and generally on any matter relevant to the operation, of this Act; (b) to monitor and enforce compliance by— (i) public and private bodies with the provisions of this Act; (ii) undertaking research into, and monitoring developments in, information processing and computer technology to ensure that any adverse effects of such developments on the protection of the personal information of data subjects are minimised, and reporting to the Minister the results of such research and monitoring; (iii) examining any proposed legislation, including subordinate legislation, or proposed policy of the Government that the Regulator considers may affect the protection of the personal information of data subjects, and reporting to the Minister the results of that examination; (iv) reporting upon request or on its own accord, to Parliament from time to time on any policy matter affecting the protection of the personal information of a data subject, including the need for, or desirability of, taking legislative, administrative, or other action to give protection or better protection to the personal information of a data subject; (v) submitting a report to Parliament, within five months of the end of its financial year, on all its activities in terms of this Act during that financial year; (vi) conducting an assessment, on its own initiative or when requested to do so, of a public or private body, in respect of the processing of personal information by that body for the purpose of ascertaining whether or not the information is processed according to the conditions for the lawful processing of personal information; (vii) monitoring the use of unique identifiers of data subjects, and reporting to Parliament from time to time on the ascertaining whether or not the information is processed according to the conditions for the lawful processing of personal information; (viii) maintaining, publishing and making available and providing copies of such registers as are prescribed in this Act; and (ix) examining any proposed legislation that makes provision for the— (aa) collection of personal information by any public or private body; or (bb) disclosure of personal information by one public or private body to any other public or private body, or both, to have particular regard, in the course of that examination, to the matters set out in section 44(2), in any case where the Regulator considers that the information might be used for the purposes of an information matching programme, and reporting to the Minister and Parliament the results of that examination; (c) to consult with interested parties by— (i) receiving and inviting representations from members of the public on any matter affecting the personal information of a data subject; (ii) co-operating on a national and international basis with other persons and bodies concerned with the protection of personal information; and (iii) acting as mediator between opposing parties on any matter that concerns the need for, or the desirability of, action by a responsible party in the interests of the protection of the personal information of a data subject; (d) to handle complaints by— (i) receiving and investigating complaints about alleged violations of the protection of personal information of data subjects and reporting to complainants in respect of such complaints; (ii) gathering such information as in the Regulator's opinion will assist the Regulator in discharging the duties and carrying out the Regulator's functions under this Act; (iii) attempting to resolve complaints by means of dispute resolution mechanisms such as mediation and conciliation; and (iv) serving any notices in terms of this Act and further promoting the resolution of disputes in accordance with the prescripts of this Act; (e) to conduct research and to report to Parliament— (i) from time to time on the desirability of the acceptance, by South Africa, of any international instrument relating to the protection of the personal information of a data subject; and (ii) on any other matter, including necessary legislative amendments, relating to protection of personal information that, in the Regulator's opinion, should be drawn to Parliament's attention; (f) in respect of codes of conduct to— (i) issue, from time to time, codes of conduct, amend codes and to revoke codes of conduct; (ii) make guidelines to assist bodies to develop codes of conduct or to apply codes of conduct; and (iii) consider afresh, upon application, determinations by adjudicators under approved codes of conduct; (g) to facilitate cross-border cooperation in the enforcement of privacy laws by participating in any initiative that is aimed at such cooperation; and (h) in general to— (i) do anything incidental or conducive to the performance of any of the preceding functions; (ii) exercise and perform such other functions, powers, and duties as are conferred or imposed on the Regulator by or under this Act or any other legislation; (iii) require the responsible party to disclose to any person affected by a compromise to the integrity or confidentiality of personal information, such compromise in accordance with section 22; and (iv) exercise the powers conferred upon the Regulator by this Act in matters relating to the access of information as provided by the Promotion of Access to Information Act.

sections.heading: (1) (a) The Regulator consists of the following members:
sections.title: Regulator composition and appointment requirements
sections.summary: This section specifies the composition of the Regulator, requiring a Chairperson and four ordinary members. Members must be appropriately qualified, with at least one being a practicing advocate, attorney, or law professor. The Chairperson must be full-time and may not perform other remunerative work without prior consent. Ordinary members must be appointed in full-time or part-time capacities, with full-time members similarly restricted from other work. The Regulator must be appointed by the President on National Assembly recommendation, with members serving up to five years.

sections.summary: Individuals working for or under the direction of the South African Regulator must treat personal information obtained during official duties as confidential, except when required by law or necessary for their official duties. sections.heading: (1) The responsible party must obtain prior authorisation from the Regulator, in
sections.title: Prior authorisation for high-risk processing
sections.summary: Responsible parties must obtain prior authorisation from the Regulator before processing unique identifiers for purposes other than their original intent, linking information across entities, criminal behavior data on behalf of third parties, credit reporting data, or special personal information of children transferred to foreign countries without adequate data protection. This authorisation is required for specific high-risk processing activities that could impact data subjects' rights.
sections.dtia_summary: This section requires prior authorisation for cross-border transfers of sensitive data, including children's information and special personal information, to countries without adequate data protection frameworks. It mandates specific safeguards for high-risk transfers to ensure compliance with POPIA.

sections.source_quote: 57. (1) The responsible party must obtain prior authorisation from the Regulator, in terms of section 58, prior to any processing if that responsible party plans to— (a) process any unique identifiers of data subjects— (i) for a purpose other than the one for which the identifier was specifically intended at collection; and (ii) with the aim of linking the information together with information processed by other responsible parties; (b) process information on criminal behaviour or on unlawful or objectionable conduct on behalf of third parties; (c) process information for the purposes of credit reporting; or (d) transfer special personal information, as referred to in section 26, or the personal information of children as referred to in section 34, to a third party in a foreign country that does not provide an adequate level of protection for the processing of personal information as referred to in section 72.

sections.heading: (1) The processing of personal information of a data subject for the purpose of
sections.title: Direct marketing: consent or customer status requirements
sections.summary: Direct marketing via electronic communications is prohibited unless the data subject has given consent or is a customer of the responsible party. Responsible parties may request consent once per data subject in a prescribed manner. For customers, processing is permitted only if contact details were obtained during a product/service sale, for similar products/services, and the data subject has been given a reasonable opportunity to object. Communications must include sender details and a way to opt-out.
sections.dtia_summary: This section imposes strict conditions on direct marketing, requiring consent or customer status, which affects DTIA compliance by mandating specific consent mechanisms and customer relationship checks for electronic communications.

requirements.text: The processing of personal information of a data subject for the purpose of direct marketing by means of any form of electronic communication is prohibited unless the data subject has given his, her or its consent to the processing

The data subject must be informed, free of charge and before the information is included in the directory about the purpose of the directory and any further uses to which the directory may possibly be put
A responsible party may approach a data subject only once to request consent if the data subject has not previously withheld such consent
The data subject's consent must be requested in the prescribed manner and form

A responsible party may only process the personal information of a data subject who is a customer of the responsible party if the responsible party has obtained the contact details of the data subject in the context of the sale of a product or service

A responsible party may only process the personal information of a data subject who is a customer of the responsible party for the purpose of direct marketing of the responsible party's own similar products or services

A responsible party may only process the personal information of a data subject who is a customer of the responsible party if the data subject has been given a reasonable opportunity to object, free of charge and in a manner free of unnecessary formality, to such use of his, her or its electronic details
Any communication for the purpose of direct marketing must contain details of the identity of the sender or the person on whose behalf the communication has been sent
Any communication for the purpose of direct marketing must contain an address or other contact details to which the recipient may send a request that such communications cease

sections.source_quote: 69. (1) The processing of personal information of a data subject for the purpose of direct marketing by means of any form of electronic communication, including automatic calling machines, facsimile machines, SMSs or e-mail is prohibited unless the data subject— (a) has given his, her or its consent to the processing; or (b) is, subject to subsection (3), a customer of the responsible party. (2) (a) A responsible party may approach a data subject— (i) whose consent is required in terms of subsection (1)(a); and (ii) who has not previously withheld such consent, only once in order to request the consent of that data subject. (b) The data subject's consent must be requested in the prescribed manner and form. (3) A responsible party may only process the personal information of a data subject who is a customer of the responsible party in terms of subsection (1)(b)— (a) if the responsible party has obtained the contact details of the data subject in the context of the sale of a product or service; (b) for the purpose of direct marketing of the responsible party's own similar products or services; and (c) if the data subject has been given a reasonable opportunity to object, free of charge and in a manner free of unnecessary formality, to such use of his, her or its electronic details— (i) at the time when the information was collected; and (ii) on the occasion of each communication with the data subject for the purpose of marketing if the data subject has not initially refused such use. (4) Any communication for the purpose of direct marketing must contain— (a) details of the identity of the sender or the person on whose behalf the communication has been sent; and (b) an address or other contact details to which the recipient may send a request that such communications cease.

sections.title: Automated decision making: legal consequences and safeguards
sections.summary: A data subject may not be subject to decisions with legal consequences or substantial effects based solely on automated profiling of personal information, such as work performance, creditworthiness, reliability, location, health, preferences, or conduct. The decision must not be taken in connection with the conclusion or execution of a contract if the request of the data subject in terms of the contract has been met

sections.source_quote: 71. (2) The provisions of subsection (1) do not apply if the decision— (a) has been taken in connection with the conclusion or execution of a contract, and— (i) the request of the data subject in terms of the contract has been met; or (ii) appropriate measures have been taken to protect the data subject's legitimate interests; or (b) is governed by a law or code of conduct in which appropriate measures are specified for protecting the legitimate interests of data subjects. (3) The appropriate measures, referred to in subsection (2)(a)(ii), must— (a) provide an opportunity for a data subject to make representations about a decision referred to in subsection ( 1); and (b) require a responsible party to provide a data subject with sufficient information about the underlying logic of the automated processing of the information relating to him or her to enable him or her to make representations in terms of paragraph (a).

sections.heading: (1) A police officer who is assisting a person authorised to conduct an entry and
sections.title: Police entry and search with warrants: force and procedures
sections.summary: Police officers may use reasonable force to overcome resistance during warrant-authorized entries and searches. Warrants must be executed at reasonable hours unless there are grounds to suspect evidence would be lost. Occupants must be shown warrants if present or copies left visible if absent. Seized items require receipts. Retained items must be documented but may be returned after reasonable delay. Searches must respect dignity, privacy, and freedom. Individuals must be advised of their right to legal assistance before questioning and allowed to exercise it. Self-incriminating statements are inadmissible in criminal proceedings except for perjury or specific offenses.
sections.dtia_summary: This section outlines specific conditions for lawful police searches under warrants, including force limits, occupant notifications, receipt requirements, and protections for individuals during searches. sections.heading: (1) If the Regulator—
sections.title: Regulator's information notices for compliance reporting
sections.summary: The Regulator may issue information notices requiring responsible parties to report compliance with POPIA after receiving a request under section 89 or when determining interference with data subjects' information. The notices specify reporting timelines, formats, and must include appeal rights. Responsible parties must provide reports indicating lawful processing or relevant information within a specified period, with extensions for appeals.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires responsible parties to demonstrate compliance with POPIA through reporting mechanisms; critical for assessing data handling practices during cross-border transfers or international processing.

requirements.text: Regulator may serve responsible party with information notice requiring report on compliance with POPIA within specified period
Report must indicate lawful processing of personal information or relevant information for compliance assessment
Information notice must include appeal rights as per section 97
Information notice must state receipt of request under section 89 for cases in subsection (1)(a)
Information notice must state relevance of information for compliance assessment for cases in subsection (1)(b)
Reporting period must not expire before appeal deadline
Information may be required urgently with reasons for urgency
Urgent notices cannot require compliance before 3 days from service
Information notices may not require communications between legal advisers and clients
Information notices may not require information revealing evidence of offences outside POPIA
Regulator may cancel information notices by written notice

sections.source_quote: 90. (1) If the Regulator— (a) has received a request under section 89 in respect of any processing of personal information; or (b) reasonably requires any information for the purpose of determining whether the responsible party has interfered or is interfering with the personal information of a data subject, the Regulator may serve the responsible party with an information notice requiring the responsible party to furnish the Regulator, within a specified period, in a form specified in the notice, with a report indicating that the processing is taking place in compliance with the provisions of the Act, or with such information relating to the request or to compliance with the Act as is so specified.
sections.relevance_reason: Regulator reporting requirements for compliance

sections.heading: (1) After completing the assessment referred to in section 89 the Regulator—
sections.title: Regulator's post-assessment reporting obligations
sections.summary: After completing assessments under section 89, the Regulator must report results and recommendations to responsible parties. The Regulator may require timely implementation of recommendations or explanations for non-action. Reports are treated as enforcement notices under section 95, allowing public disclosure if in the public interest.
sections.dtia_summary: Mandates transparency in compliance assessments; critical for DTIA as it requires responsible parties to demonstrate corrective actions for data protection breaches.

requirements.text: Regulator must report assessment results and recommendations to responsible party
Regulator may require responsible party to inform on actions taken or proposed to implement recommendations
Regulator may make public information on responsible party's practices if in public interest
Regulator report is deemed equivalent to enforcement notice under section 95

sections.source_quote: 91. (1) After completing the assessment referred to in section 89 the Regulator— (a) must report to the responsible party the results of the assessment and any recommendations that the Regulator considers appropriate; and (b) may, in appropriate cases, require the responsible party, within a specified time, to inform the Regulator of any action taken or proposed to be taken to implement the recommendations contained in the report or reasons why no such action has been or is proposed to be taken.
sections.relevance_reason: Regulator's enforcement reporting obligations

sections.heading: (1) After completing the investigation of a complaint or other matter in terms of
sections.title: Regulator's referral to Enforcement Committee
sections.summary: The Regulator may refer complaints or matters to the Enforcement Committee for findings and recommendations on actions. The Committee can prescribe procedures for submissions, legal representation, timelines for decisions, and urgent matters handling. This section enables structured resolution of complex compliance issues.
sections.dtia_summary: Provides a formal mechanism for resolving compliance disputes; relevant for DTIA as it outlines procedures for handling complaints and referrals that may impact data transfer or processing.

requirements.text: Regulator may refer complaint or matter to Enforcement Committee for consideration
Enforcement Committee must make finding on referred matter
Enforcement Committee may make recommendations for action against responsible party
Regulator may prescribe procedure for submissions to Enforcement Committee
Regulator may prescribe opportunity for legal representation in Enforcement Committee
Regulator may prescribe timeline for Enforcement Committee to make finding
Enforcement Committee may finalise urgent matters

sections.source_quote: 92. (1) After completing the investigation of a complaint or other matter in terms of this Act, the Regulator may refer such complaint or other matter to the Enforcement Committee for consideration, a finding in respect of the complaint or other matter and a recommendation in respect of the proposed action to be taken by the Regulator as referred to in section 93.
sections.relevance_reason: Regulator's referral process for compliance resolution

sections.heading: The Enforcement Committee—
sections.title: Enforcement Committee's duties and recommendations
sections.summary: The Enforcement Committee must consider matters referred by the Regulator and make findings. It may recommend actions against responsible parties or information officers under POPIA, including enforcement measures. This section ensures accountability through structured committee processes.
sections.dtia_summary: Critical for DTIA as it defines the committee's role in enforcing compliance, impacting how data transfers are assessed and corrected.

requirements.text: Enforcement Committee must consider matters referred by Regulator
Enforcement Committee must make finding on referred matter
Enforcement Committee may make recommendations for action against responsible party
Enforcement Committee may make recommendations for action against information officers

sections.source_quote: 93. The Enforcement Committee— (a) must consider all matters referred to it by the Regulator in terms of section 92 or the Promotion of Access to Information Act and make a finding in respect thereof; and (b) may make any recommendation to the Regulator necessary or incidental to any action that should be taken against— (i) a responsible party in terms of this Act; or (ii) an information officer or head of a private body, as the case may be, in terms of the Promotion of Access to Information Act.
sections.relevance_reason: Enforcement Committee's accountability for POPIA compliance

sections.heading: If an investigation is made following a complaint, and—
sections.title: Regulator's notification of investigation outcomes
sections.summary: The Regulator must inform complainants and responsible parties of investigation developments and results when specific conditions are met, including no interference, referral to the Enforcement Committee, enforcement notices, or appeals. This ensures transparency in complaint resolution processes.
sections.dtia_summary: Ensures timely communication of investigation outcomes; vital for DTIA to track compliance status and potential impacts on data transfers.

requirements.text: Regulator must inform complainant and responsible party of investigation developments
Regulator must inform complainant and responsible party of investigation results

sections.source_quote: 94. If an investigation is made following a complaint, and— (a) the Regulator believes that no interference with the protection of the personal information of a data subject has taken place and therefore does not serve an enforcement notice; (b) the Regulator has referred the complaint to the Enforcement Committee for consideration in terms of section 92; (c) an enforcement notice is served in terms of section 95; (d) a served enforcement notice is cancelled in terms of section 96; (e) an appeal is lodged against the enforcement notice for cancellation or variation of the notice in terms of section 97; or (f) an appeal against an enforcement notice is allowed, the notice is substituted or the appeal is dismissed in terms of section 98, the Regulator must inform the complainant and the responsible party, as soon as reasonably practicable, in the manner prescribed of any development mentioned in paragraphs (a) to (f) and the result of the investigation.
sections.relevance_reason: Regulator's notification of investigation outcomes

sections.heading: (1) If the Regulator, after having considered the recommendation of the
sections.title: Enforcement notices for data protection breaches
sections.summary: The Regulator may issue enforcement notices requiring responsible parties to take corrective actions or stop processing personal information if interference with data protection is found. Notices must specify interference details, appeal rights, and allow extensions for appeals. Urgent notices cannot require compliance before 3 days.
sections.dtia_summary: Directly impacts DTIA by mandating specific corrective actions for data breaches; critical for assessing and mitigating cross-border data transfer risks.

requirements.text: Regulator may serve enforcement notice requiring responsible party to take specified steps or refrain from actions
Regulator may serve enforcement notice requiring responsible party to stop processing personal information
Enforcement notice must indicate nature of interference and reasons for conclusion
Enforcement notice must include appeal rights as per section 97
Compliance period must not expire before appeal deadline
Urgent enforcement notices may include statement of urgency and reasons
Urgent enforcement notices cannot require compliance before 3 days from service

sections.source_quote: 95. (1) If the Regulator, after having considered the recommendation of the Enforcement Committee in terms of section 93, is satisfied that a responsible party has interfered or is interfering with the protection of the personal information of a data subject as referred to in section 73, the Regulator may serve the responsible party with an enforcement notice requiring the responsible party to do either or both of the following: (a) To take specified steps within a period specified in the notice, or to refrain from taking such steps; or (b) to stop processing personal information specified in the notice, or to stop processing personal information for a purpose or in a manner specified in the notice within a period specified in the notice.
sections.relevance_reason: core enforcement mechanism for data breaches

sections.heading: (1) A responsible party on whom an enforcement notice has been served may, at
sections.title: Appeal for enforcement notice cancellation after appeal period
sections.summary: A responsible party served with an enforcement notice may apply to the Regulator for cancellation or variation of the notice after the appeal period expires, if changes in circumstances mean the notice no longer ensures lawful processing of personal information. The Regulator may then cancel or vary the notice if it determines the notice no longer aligns with lawful processing conditions.
sections.dtia_summary: Allows responsible parties to seek removal of enforcement notices if circumstances change, relevant for DTIA when assessing whether notices remain necessary for lawful processing after appeals.

requirements.text: A responsible party may apply to the Regulator for cancellation or variation of an enforcement notice after the appeal period expires
The Regulator may cancel or vary the notice if it determines the notice no longer ensures lawful processing of personal information

sections.source_quote: A responsible party on whom an enforcement notice has been served may, at any time after the expiry of the period during which an appeal may be brought against that notice, apply in writing to the Regulator for the cancellation or variation of that notice on the ground that, by reason of a change of circumstances, all or any of the provisions of that notice need not be complied with in order to ensure compliance with the conditions for the lawful processing of personal information.
sections.relevance_reason: Regulator's authority to cancel enforcement notices post-appeal

sections.heading: (1) A responsible party on whom an information or enforcement notice has been
sections.title: Appeal process for enforcement notices and investigation results
sections.summary: A responsible party may appeal an enforcement notice to the High Court within 30 days of receiving it. A complainant may also appeal the Regulator's investigation result within 180 days of receiving it. Both appeals aim to set aside or vary the notice or decision.
sections.dtia_summary: Provides timelines for appealing enforcement notices and investigation results, critical for DTIA when assessing appeal rights and timelines in cross-border data transfer contexts.

requirements.text: A responsible party may appeal to the High Court within 30 days of receiving an enforcement notice
A complainant may appeal the Regulator's investigation result within 180 days of receiving it

sections.source_quote: A responsible party on whom an information or enforcement notice has been served may, within 30 days of receiving the notice, appeal to the High Court having jurisdiction for the setting aside or variation of the notice.
sections.relevance_reason: Appeal process for enforcement notices

sections.heading: (1) If in an appeal under section 97 the court considers—
sections.title: Court remedies for appeals against enforcement notices
sections.summary: If a court finds an enforcement notice unlawful or the Regulator exercised discretion incorrectly, it must allow the appeal and may set aside the notice or substitute a new one. The court may also review factual determinations underlying the notice.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines court remedies for appeals against enforcement notices, relevant for DTIA when assessing judicial oversight of enforcement actions in cross-border data transfers.

requirements.text: The court must allow the appeal if it finds the notice or decision unlawful or the Regulator exercised discretion incorrectly
The court may set aside the notice or substitute a new notice
The court may review factual determinations underlying the notice

sections.source_quote: If in an appeal under section 97 the court considers that the notice or decision against which the appeal is brought is not in accordance with the law; or that the notice or decision involved an exercise of discretion by the Regulator that ought to have been exercised differently, the court must allow the appeal and may set aside the notice or substitute such other notice or decision as should have been served or made by the Regulator.
sections.relevance_reason: Court remedies for appeals against enforcement notices

sections.heading: (1)Adata subject or, at the request of the data subject, the Regulator, may institute
sections.title: Civil remedies for personal information breaches
sections.summary: Data subjects or the Regulator may sue responsible parties for damages in court for breaches of the Act. Responsible parties can raise defenses including vis major, consent, plaintiff fault, impracticability, or Regulator exemptions. Courts may award damages, aggravated damages, interest, and costs, with funds deposited into a trust account for distribution to data subjects.
sections.dtia_summary: Outlines civil remedies for data breaches, relevant for DTIA when assessing liability and compensation mechanisms in cross-border data transfers.

requirements.text: A data subject or Regulator may institute civil action for damages against a responsible party for breach of the Act
The responsible party may raise defenses including vis major, consent, plaintiff fault, impracticability, or Regulator exemptions
Courts may award damages, aggravated damages, interest, and costs
Awarded funds must be deposited into a trust account for distribution to data subjects
The Regulator may recover reasonable expenses from the trust account
Any undistributed funds after three years accrue to the Regulator

sections.source_quote: A data subject or, at the request of the data subject, the Regulator, may institute a civil action for damages in a court having jurisdiction against a responsible party for breach of any provision of this Act as referred to in section 73, whether or not there is intent or negligence on the part of the responsible party.
sections.relevance_reason: Breach remedies and data subject rights

sections.heading: Any person who hinders, obstructs or unlawfully influences the Regulator or any
sections.title: Obstruction of Regulator duties
sections.summary: Any person who hinders, obstructs, or unlawfully influences the Regulator or its agents in performing duties under the Act commits an offence. This includes interference with regulatory processes and enforcement actions.
sections.dtia_summary: Addresses obstruction of regulatory duties, relevant for DTIA when assessing compliance with regulatory oversight in cross-border data transfers.

requirements.text: Any person who hinders, obstructs or unlawfully influences the Regulator or any person acting on behalf of the Regulator in the performance of the Regulator's duties and functions under this Act is guilty of an offence

sections.source_quote: Any person who hinders, obstructs or unlawfully influences the Regulator or any person acting on behalf of or under the direction of the Regulator in the performance of the Regulator's duties and functions under this Act, is guilty of an offence.
sections.relevance_reason: obstruction of regulator duties

sections.heading: Any person who—
sections.title: Failure to assist warrant execution
sections.summary: A person who intentionally obstructs warrant execution or fails to provide reasonable assistance to warrant executors commits an offence. This relates to law enforcement warrants under the Act.
sections.dtia_summary: Addresses legal obligations regarding warrant execution, relevant for DTIA when assessing compliance with law enforcement procedures in cross-border data transfers.

requirements.text: A person who intentionally obstructs a warrant execution is guilty of an offence
A person who fails without reasonable excuse to assist warrant execution is guilty of an offence

sections.source_quote: Any person who— (a) intentionally obstructs a person in the execution of a warrant issued under section 82; or (b) fails without reasonable excuse to give any person executing such a warrant such assistance as he or she may reasonably require for the execution of the warrant, is guilty of an offence.
sections.relevance_reason: enforcement of warrants affects data access

sections.heading: (1) A responsible party which fails to comply with an enforcement notice served
sections.title: Offence for non-compliance with enforcement notices
sections.summary: A responsible party that fails to comply with an enforcement notice issued under section 95 commits an offence. This section also covers offences for knowingly or recklessly making false statements in response to information notices under section 90. The offences apply to responsible parties and are punishable by fines or imprisonment.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes criminal liability for non-compliance with regulatory notices, which impacts DTIA by requiring thorough assessment of compliance with enforcement actions and information notices.

requirements.text: A responsible party which fails to comply with an enforcement notice served in terms of section 95 is guilty of an offence
A responsible party which makes a statement knowing it to be false in response to an information notice under section 90 is guilty of an offence
A responsible party which recklessly makes a statement which is false in a material respect in response to an information notice under section 90 is guilty of an offence

sections.source_quote: 103. (1) A responsible party which fails to comply with an enforcement notice served in terms of section 95, is guilty of an offence. (2) A responsible party which, in purported compliance with an information notice served in terms of section 90— (a) makes a statement knowing it to be false; or (b) recklessly makes a statement which is false, in a material respect, is guilty of an offence.
sections.relevance_reason: Offence for non-compliance with enforcement notices

sections.heading: (1) Any person summoned in terms of section 81 to attend and give evidence or
sections.title: Offence for failure to attend court proceedings
sections.summary: A person summoned by the Regulator to attend court proceedings or produce documents may commit an offence by failing to attend at the specified time, refusing to be sworn, giving false evidence, or failing to produce required documents. This section also covers offences for providing false evidence after being sworn in.
sections.dtia_summary: This section defines offences related to court proceedings, which may impact DTIA by requiring assessments of compliance with regulatory summons and evidence procedures.

requirements.text: A person summoned to attend and give evidence or produce documents before the Regulator who, without sufficient cause fails to attend at the time and place specified in the summons is guilty of an offence

A person summoned to attend and give evidence or produce documents before the Regulator who, without sufficient cause fails to remain in attendance until conclusion of proceedings is guilty of an offence

A person summoned to attend and give evidence or produce documents before the Regulator who, without sufficient cause refuses to be sworn or make an affirmation after being required by the Chairperson is guilty of an offence

A person summoned to attend and give evidence or produce documents before the Regulator who, without sufficient cause fails to answer fully and satisfactorily any question lawfully put is guilty of an offence

A person summoned to attend and give evidence or produce documents before the Regulator who, without sufficient cause fails to produce any book, document or object in his or her possession or custody is guilty of an offence
A person who after being sworn or making an affirmation gives false evidence before the Regulator on any matter knowing it to be false is guilty of an offence

sections.source_quote: 104. (1) Any person summoned in terms of section 81 to attend and give evidence or to produce any book, document or object before the Regulator who, without sufficient cause fails— (a) to attend at the time and place specified in the summons; (b) to remain in attendance until conclusion of the proceedings or until he or she is excused by the Chairperson of the Regulator from further attendance; (c) having attended, refuses to be sworn or to make an affirmation as witness after he or she has been required by the Chairperson of the Regulator to do so; (d) having been sworn or having made an affirmation, to answer fully and satisfactorily any question lawfully put to him or her; or (e) to produce any book, document or object in his or her possession or custody or under his or her control, which he or she has been summoned to produce, is guilty of an offence. (2) Any person who after having been sworn or having made an affirmation, gives false evidence before the Regulator on any matter, knowing such evidence to be false or not knowing or believing it to be true, is guilty of an offence.
sections.relevance_reason: irrelevant: court proceedings, evidence, not data compliance

sections.heading: (1) A responsible party who contravenes the provisions of section 8 insofar as
sections.title: Offence for misuse of account numbers
sections.summary: A responsible party that contravenes section 8 regarding account numbers is guilty of an offence if the contravention is serious or persistent and likely to cause substantial damage or distress to data subjects. The responsible party must have known or ought to have known about the risk of the contravention and failed to take reasonable steps to prevent it. Account numbers are defined as unique identifiers for accessing funds or credit facilities.
sections.dtia_summary: This section specifies offences related to account number misuse, which impacts DTIA by requiring assessments of account number handling and safeguards against misuse.

requirements.text: A responsible party who contravenes section 8 insofar as those provisions relate to the processing of an account number is guilty of an offence if the contravention is of a serious or persistent nature

A responsible party who contravenes section 8 insofar as those provisions relate to the processing of an account number is guilty of an offence if the contravention likely causes substantial damage or distress to the data subject
The responsible party must have known or ought to have known that there was a risk the contravention would occur
The responsible party must have known or ought to have known that the contravention would likely cause substantial damage or distress to the data subject
The responsible party must have failed to take reasonable steps to prevent the contravention
A valid defence to a charge of contravening section 8 is taking all reasonable steps to comply with section 8

sections.source_quote: 105. (1) A responsible party who contravenes the provisions of section 8 insofar as those provisions relate to the processing of an account number of a data subject is, subject to subsections (2) and (3), guilty of an offence. (2) The contravention referred to in subsection (1) must— (a) be of a serious or persistent nature; and (b) likely cause substantial damage or distress to the data subject. (3) The responsible party must— (a) have known or ought to have known that— (i) there was a risk that the contravention would occur; or (ii) such contravention would likely cause substantial damage or distress to the data subject; and (b) have failed to take reasonable steps to prevent the contravention.
sections.relevance_reason: Financial data misuse offence with strict conditions

sections.heading: (1)Aperson who knowingly or recklessly, without the consent of the responsible
sections.title: Offence for unauthorized account number disclosure
sections.summary: A person who knowingly or recklessly discloses an account number without consent commits an offence. The offence also covers procuring disclosure of account numbers to others. Defences include necessary actions for law enforcement, court orders, reasonable belief of legal entitlement, or public interest. Selling account numbers is also an offence.
sections.dtia_summary: This section addresses offences related to unauthorized account number disclosure, which impacts DTIA by requiring assessments of account number handling and disclosure protocols.

requirements.text: A person who knowingly or recklessly obtains or discloses an account number without consent of the responsible party is guilty of an offence
A person who procures the disclosure of an account number to another person without consent of the responsible party is guilty of an offence
A valid defence to an offence under subsection (1) is that the obtaining, disclosure or procuring was necessary for law enforcement purposes
A valid defence to an offence under subsection (1) is that the obtaining, disclosure or procuring was required or authorized by law or court order
A valid defence to an offence under subsection (1) is that the person acted in reasonable belief of legal entitlement
A valid defence to an offence under subsection (1) is that the person acted in reasonable belief that consent would have been obtained if the responsible party had known
A valid defence to an offence under subsection (1) is that the obtaining, disclosure or procuring was in the public interest
A person who sells an account number obtained in contravention of subsection (1) is guilty of an offence
A person who offers to sell an account number obtained in contravention of subsection (1) is guilty of an offence

sections.source_quote: 106. (1) A person who knowingly or recklessly, without the consent of the responsible party— (a) obtains or discloses an account number of a data subject; or (b) procures the disclosure of an account number of a data subject to another person, is, subject to subsection (2), guilty of an offence.
sections.relevance_reason: Financial data breach, unauthorized disclosure

sections.heading: Any person convicted of an offence in terms of this Act, is liable, in the case of
sections.title: Penalties for offences under POPIA
sections.summary: A person convicted of an offence under POPIA faces penalties including fines up to R10 million or imprisonment for up to 10 years for certain sections, and up to 12 months for others. The penalties apply to specific sections of the Act, with distinctions based on the severity of the offence.
sections.dtia_summary: This section details penalties for POPIA offences, which impacts DTIA by requiring assessments of potential penalties for non-compliance with data protection obligations.

requirements.text: A person convicted of an offence under section 100, 103(1), 104(2), 105(1), 106(1), (3) or (4) is liable to a fine or imprisonment for up to 10 years
A person convicted of an offence under section 59, 101, 102, 103(2) or 104(1) is liable to a fine or imprisonment for up to 12 months

sections.source_quote: 107. Any person convicted of an offence in terms of this Act, is liable, in the case of a contravention of— (a) section 100, 103(1), 104(2), 105(1), 106(1), (3) or (4) to a fine or to imprisonment for a period not exceeding 10 years, or to both a fine and such imprisonment; or (b) section 59, 101, 102, 103(2) or 104(1), to a fine or to imprisonment for a period not exceeding 12 months, or to both a fine and such imprisonment.
sections.relevance_reason: Penalties for POPIA offences directly impact data compliance

sections.heading: (1) If a responsible party is alleged to have committed an offence in terms of this
sections.title: Infringement notices for offences
sections.summary: The Regulator may issue infringement notices to responsible parties for alleged offences, specifying the amount of an administrative fine up to R10 million. The notice allows the infringer to pay the fine, pay in installments, or elect court proceedings. The Regulator considers factors like the nature of information, extent of contravention, and potential damage when determining fines.
sections.dtia_summary: This section outlines the process for administrative fines, which impacts DTIA by requiring assessments of potential fines for non-compliance with data protection obligations.

requirements.text: The Regulator may deliver an infringement notice to a responsible party for alleged offences

The infringement notice must specify the particulars of the alleged offence

The infringer may pay the administrative fine within 30 days of service
The infringer may make arrangements to pay the administrative fine in installments within 30 days of service
The infringer may elect to be tried in court on a charge of the alleged offence
Failure to comply with the infringement notice within the permitted time results in the fine becoming recoverable

The Regulator must consider the duration and extent of the contravention when determining fines
The Regulator must consider the number of data subjects affected by the contravention when determining fines
The Regulator must consider whether the contravention raises public importance when determining fines
The Regulator must consider the likelihood of substantial damage or distress from the contravention when determining fines
The Regulator must consider whether the responsible party could have prevented the contravention when determining fines
The Regulator must consider failures in risk assessment or good practices when determining fines
The Regulator must consider previous offences by the responsible party when determining fines

sections.source_quote: 109. (1) If a responsible party is alleged to have committed an offence in terms of this Act, the Regulator may cause to be delivered by hand to that person (hereinafter referred to as the infringer) an infringement notice which must contain the particulars contemplated in subsection (2). (2) A notice referred to in subsection (1) must— (a) specify the name and address of the infringer; (b) specify the particulars of the alleged offence; (c) specify the amount of the administrative fine payable, which amount may, subject to subsection (10), not exceed R10 million; (d) inform the infringer that, not later than 30 days after the date of service of the infringement notice, the infringer may— (i) pay the administrative fine; (ii) make arrangements with the Regulator to pay the administrative fine in instalments; or (iii) elect to be tried in court on a charge of having committed the alleged offence referred to in terms of this Act; and (e) state that a failure to comply with the requirements of the notice within the time permitted, will result in the administrative fine becoming recoverable as contemplated in subsection (5).

sections.title: Fees for data subjects to responsible parties and Regulator
sections.summary: The Minister may prescribe fees payable by data subjects to responsible parties and the Regulator, with different fee categories for different responsible parties and data subjects. sections.source_quote: 112. (1) The Minister may, subject to section 113, make regulations relating to— (a) the establishment of the Regulator; and (b) fees referred to in section 111(1). (2) The Regulator may, subject to section 113, make regulations relating to— (a) the manner in terms of which a data subject may object to the processing of personal information as referred to in section 11(3); (b) the manner in which a data subject may submit a request to a responsible party as referred to in section 24(1); (c) the processing of health information by certain responsible parties as referred to in section 32(6); (d) the responsibilities of information officers as referred to in section 55(1)(e); (e) the form in terms of which an application for a code of conduct must be submitted to the Regulator as referred to in section 61(1)(b); (f) the manner and form within which the data subject's consent must be requested as referred to in section 69(2); (g) the manner and form in terms of which a complaint must be submitted in terms of section 74; (h) the Regulator acting as conciliator in relation to any interference with the protection of personal information as referred to in section 76(1)(b); (i) the notification of the parties concerned of an investigation to be conducted as referred to in section 79; (j) the settlement of complaints as referred to in section 80; (k) the manner in which an assessment of the processing of personal information will be made as referred to in section 89(1); (l) the manner in terms of which the parties concerned must be informed of the developments during and result of an investigation as referred to in section 94; and (m) matters incidental to the imposition of administrative fines as referred to in section 109.

sections.heading: (1) The Minister, before making or amending any regulations referred to in
sections.title: Regulation publication and consultation process
sections.summary: The Minister must publish a notice in the Gazette before making or amending regulations, specifying draft regulations, where they can be obtained, and inviting written comments within a specified period. After consultation with the Regulator, the Minister may amend regulations and publish them in final form. sections.heading: The amendment of section 55 by the
sections.title: Amendment of section 55 for credit regulator consultation
sections.summary: This section amends section 55 to require the National Credit Regulator to consult with the regulatory authority that issued a license to a regulated financial institution before issuing a notice to that institution. It also specifies that certain sections of the Act are subject to compliance procedures outlined in Chapters 10 and 11.
sections.dtia_summary: This section affects how credit institutions handle data transfers, requiring consultation with regulators before certain actions and aligning with compliance procedures for financial data.

Sections 68, 70(1), (2)(b) to (g) and (i), (3) and (4) and 72(1), (3) and (5) are subject to compliance procedures in Chapters 10 and 11

sections.title: Amendment of section 136 for complaint submission
sections.summary: This section amends section 136 of the Act to allow any person to submit a complaint about alleged contraventions to the National Credit Regulator, provided they follow the prescribed manner and form as specified in section 55(2)(b). The complaint process is subject to the requirements outlined in section :55(2)(b).
